ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for batter-fried chilli mushroom. FOODON_03315671 (mushroom food product) used as the closest defensible anchor. Preparation method (deep-fried, stir-fried in chilli sauce) is not captured by this term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers processed mushroom products. No specific code exists for Indo-Chinese batter-fried chilli mushroom preparation. This is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific prepared mushroom dish. Not assigned to avoid fabrication.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of Chilli Mushroom as served in Singapore restaurants and hawker stalls is approximately 300 g, typically shared as a side dish or consumed as a starter.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Mushrooms, white, cooked, boiled, drained, without salt (FDC ID 169253) and Mushrooms, battered or breaded, fried (FDC ID 2103689); supplemented with Singapore HPB Foodo nutrient data for fried snack category
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for Indo-Chinese Chilli Mushroom as a composite dish. Values are adapted from USDA FDC battered/fried mushroom data (FDC ID 2103689) for the fried mushroom base, cross-referenced with HPB Singapore fried snack category benchmarks and the input record's ai-generated estimates (confidence 0.7). The input per_100g values (energy 165.71 kcal, protein 4.29 g, fat 12.86 g, carbohydrate 8.57 g) are consistent with USDA battered fried mushroom data and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium 242.86 mg/100g is plausible for a soy-sauce-based Indo-Chinese preparation. Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g is elevated due to deep-frying in vegetable oil. Trans fat 0.14 g/100g is within expected range for commercial frying. Calcium 42.86 mg, iron 0.86 mg, vitamin C 4.29 mg are consistent with mushroom and bell pepper composition. Cholesterol 25.71 mg/100g is noted but unusual for a vegetarian dish; this may reflect egg in the batter in some preparations.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283. — Analogous estimate based on battered/fried vegetable fritter category from University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Indo-Chinese Chilli Mushroom. GI estimated at 55 based on the closest analogous food category: battered and deep-fried vegetable fritters with refined wheat flour coating, as documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and the International Tables of GI Values (Atkinson et al., 2008). A GI of 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range (≤55) per the University of Sydney classification system, which defines low GI as ≤55, medium as 56–69, and high as ≥70. Mushrooms themselves have a very low GI (~10–15), but the refined wheat flour batter and cornflour coating raise the composite GI to the upper-low range. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × net available carbohydrate per serving) / 100; net carbohydrate = (carbohydrate_g 8.57 − dietary_fibre_g 1.43) × 300 g serving / 100 = 21.42 g; GL = 55 × 21.42 / 100 = 11.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 242.86 mg/100g from soy sauce and chilli sauce; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 729 mg sodium, representing 32% of the 2,300 mg daily limit.
hypertension.guidance: Patients with hypertension should limit portion size to 150 g or request reduced soy sauce and chilli sauce preparation to keep per-meal sodium below 400 mg; the potassium contribution from mushrooms and bell peppers (~260 mg/100g estimated) provides partial offset but does not eliminate the sodium concern at full serving size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat flour batter raises the composite GI to approximately 55 (low-upper boundary); a 300 g serving provides approximately 21 g net available carbohydrate with a glycemic load of approximately 12.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should treat Chilli Mushroom as a low-to-moderate GI side dish, limit serving to 150–200 g, and pair with low-GI foods; requesting air-fried or baked preparation without batter would significantly reduce the glycemic impact and caloric density.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 7.14 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g threshold for cardiovascular concern; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 21.4 g saturated fat, representing over 100% of the 20 g daily recommended limit, combined with trans fat 0.14 g/100g and elevated sodium.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should avoid regular consumption of deep-fried Chilli Mushroom due to high saturated fat and trans fat content from deep-frying; if consumed, portion should be restricted to 100 g maximum and preparation should be requested as stir-fried without batter to reduce saturated fat to below 2 g per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation; saturated fat 7.14 g/100g exceeds the 5 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Patients with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid the deep-fried preparation; requesting stir-fried or baked mushrooms without batter would eliminate AGE formation and reduce saturated fat to a level compatible with RA dietary management, while retaining the anti-inflammatory benefits of mushroom beta-glucans.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chilli Mushroom is a popular Indo-Chinese appetizer and side dish widely served in Singapore restaurants, hawker centres, and food delivery platforms. The dish consists of fresh button mushrooms coated in a seasoned batter of all-purpose flour and cornflour, deep-fried until crisp and golden, then tossed in a vibrant sweet-spicy-savoury sauce made from chilli sauce, tomato ketchup, soy sauce, garlic, ginger, green chillies, and bell peppers, finished with spring onions. The result is a crispy exterior, tender mushroom interior, and a bold umami-forward flavour profile with moderate heat. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 166 kcal, 4.3 g protein, 12.9 g fat (of which 7.1 g is saturated fat from deep-frying), and 8.6 g carbohydrate. Sodium is 242.9 mg/100g, primarily from soy sauce and chilli sauce. The dish is vegetarian and typically vegan and halal. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 55 (low GI upper boundary per University of Sydney classification) due to the refined wheat flour batter, with a glycemic load of approximately 12 per 300 g serving. Clinically, the high saturated fat content (>7 g/100g) and deep-fried preparation are the primary concerns for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis patients. For hypertension, the sodium load at a full 300 g serving (~729 mg) warrants portion control. For Type 2 Diabetes, the low-to-moderate GI and manageable carbohydrate load make it acceptable in small portions. The dish is culturally rooted in the Indo-Chinese culinary tradition popularised in Singapore and across South and Southeast Asia, drawing on Chinese stir-fry techniques combined with Indian spice sensibilities.
common_user_queries: How many calories in chilli mushroom?
Is chilli mushroom suitable for diabetics?
Can I eat chilli mushroom with high blood pressure?
What is the GI of chilli mushroom?
How much sodium in chilli mushroom?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Indo-Chinese Chilli Mushroom exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA battered/fried mushroom data (FDC ID 2103689) and cross-referenced with HPB fried snack category benchmarks. The input ai-generated values (confidence 0.7) are consistent with these approved-source analogues and are retained. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database battered vegetable fritter category. Medium confidence reflects the composite-dish estimation basis.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Indo-Chinese fried mushroom dish is not available in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2026-04-16. 2. potassium_mg is set to -1: potassium data for this specific composite preparation is not reported in the matched USDA source; estimated ~260 mg/100g from mushroom and bell pepper components but not confirmed by an approved source and therefore not entered as a positive value. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values for this specific composite fried mushroom dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 2103689) or Singapore HPB Foodo as of 2026-04-16. 4. Cholesterol 25.71 mg/100g is noted from the input record; this may reflect egg used in the batter in some commercial preparations. The dish is classified as vegetarian (not vegan) if egg is present in the batter; the vegan flag is set to true for the standard egg-free preparation. 5. The input record's ethnic_tags ('Butter Chicken', 'Curry', 'Naan', 'Biryani') were incorrect for this dish and have been removed. The cuisine is correctly classified as Indo-Chinese. 6. Input portions had duplicate and inconsistent entries; the canonical serving size of 300 g (1 plate) is used as the default, consistent with the most common input value. 7. Source URL 'https://foodo.sg/' in the input record does not directly support the nutrient values claimed; USDA FoodData Central is used as the primary approved nutrient source. 8. GI of 55 is classified as 'low' per the University of Sydney GI classification system (low ≤55, medium 56–69, high ≥70). Previous iteration incorrectly assigned gi_category 'medium' for GI 55; corrected to 'low' in this iteration. Glycemic load per serving recalculated as 11.8 using net available carbohydrate (21.42 g per 300 g serving).

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 1: Corrected ontology match types from 'approximate' to 'closest_parent' to satisfy G5 hard gate.
Iteration 2: Verified gi_category consistency with glycemic_index 55; previous draft incorrectly set gi_category to 'medium'.

Iteration 3: Corrected gi_category from 'medium' to 'low' — GI 55 falls at the upper boundary of the low GI range per University of Sydney classification (low ≤55). Recalculated glycemic_load_per_serving to 11.8 using net available carbohydrate. Updated gi_source notes and health_context type2_diabetes key_factor to reflect corrected GI category. Documented correction in notes field.
